methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Late Stage → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.2x) to $5.7B last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Transportation (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 2 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 4 same-sector peers exist in Transportation, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
The Boring Company is a late-stage Transportation company currently valued at $5.7B post-money, having raised $675M in aggregate capital. The Transportation sector comp universe consists of four private peers (Rivian, Einride, Harbinger, REGENT Craft) with no public comparables identified. A narrow peer-median approach proved inapplicable due to insufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage peers meeting the three-datapoint threshold.

The Stage-Progression method was the sole applicable triangulation approach. Applied to the $5.7B last post-money valuation, a 1.2x step-up multiplier typical for late-stage advancement yields $6.8B. This method directly reflects the company's capital-raising trajectory and current position within the late-stage funding continuum.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $5.8B to $7.9B, with a median of $6.8B. The range anchors at the company's last financing round ($5.8B floor) and extends upward based on standard late-stage progression dynamics. Key uncertainties limiting conviction include the absence of a sufficiently deep peer set with comparable stage and sector positioning—only one method triangulated successfully—and dependence on historical step-up multiples that may not fully account for sector-specific market conditions or company-specific execution risk. Widening the analysis would require either identification of additional same-stage Transportation peers or expansion to adjacent private infrastructure sectors.
